title: What to know about Entada phaseoloides  (fish bean) before using it medicinally
section_medicine_ailments: Entada phaseoloides helps healing several common ailments, such as fever, cough, and respiratory issues. The plant has been traditionally used in various cultures to treat a range of health conditions, including bronchitis, asthma, and pneumonia. Its anti-inflammatory properties make it effective in reducing inflammation in the lungs and airways, providing relief from congestion and difficulty breathing. Additionally, its antimicrobial properties help combat bacterial and viral infections that cause respiratory tract infections. The plant's expectorant properties also aid in loosening and removing mucus and phlegm from the body, making it easier to expel and clear out the lungs, which can be particularly beneficial for patients with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). Overall, Entada phaseoloides is a valuable natural remedy that has been used for centuries to alleviate symptoms of respiratory illnesses and promote overall lung health.
section_medicine_properties: Entada phaseoloides has several medicinal properties, such as its antidiabetic, antibacterial, and anti-inflammatory effects, which have been extensively studied and documented. The most important of these is its ability to regulate blood sugar levels and alleviate symptoms associated with diabetes mellitus. Research has shown that the plant's extracts can significantly reduce blood glucose levels in both normal and diabetic individuals, making it a potential natural treatment option for type 2 diabetes. Additionally, Entada phaseoloides has been found to exhibit antibacterial activity against various pathogens, including Staphylococcus aureus, Escherichia coli, and Pseudomonas aeruginosa, suggesting its potential use in wound healing and the treatment of infections. Furthermore, its anti-inflammatory properties may help alleviate symptoms associated with inflammatory conditions such as arthritis, making it a valuable addition to traditional medicine practices. Overall, Entada phaseoloides is considered a valuable medicinal plant due to its multifaceted therapeutic effects, which make it a promising candidate for further research and development of new medicines.
section_medicine_parts: The most commonly used parts of Entada phaseoloides for medicinal purposes are its seeds, leaves, and roots. The seeds of E. phaseoloides have been traditionally used to treat a variety of ailments, including fever, cough, bronchitis, and asthma. They contain a number of bioactive compounds, including flavonoids, alkaloids, and saponins, which have been shown to possess anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant properties. The leaves of the plant are also used in traditional medicine to treat skin conditions such as eczema and dermatitis, while the roots are used to treat a range of digestive issues including diarrhea, dysentery, and constipation. Additionally, the plant's seeds have been found to exhibit anti-cancer properties, with studies suggesting that they may be effective in inhibiting the growth of certain types of cancer cells.
section_medicine_side_effects: When used improperly, Entada phaseoloides increases the chances of experiencing side effects, such as gastrointestinal disturbances, allergic reactions, and even more serious complications. If the plant's seeds are not properly prepared or dosed, they may cause stomach upset, diarrhea, and vomiting due to their high saponin content. Additionally, some individuals may experience skin irritation, itching, or rashes after coming into contact with the plant's sap or applying a preparation without proper purification. In rare cases, improper use can lead to more severe reactions, including anaphylaxis, which requires immediate medical attention. Furthermore, if Entada phaseoloides is used in combination with other medications or substances, it may interact with them in unpredictable ways, leading to adverse effects such as increased bleeding risk, altered blood pressure, or even organ damage. It is essential to consult with a qualified healthcare professional and follow proper preparation and dosing guidelines when using Entada phaseoloides for medicinal purposes to minimize the risk of these potential side effects.
section_medicine_precautions: The precautions to take before using Entada phaseoloides medicinally are crucial to ensure safe and effective use of this plant. One of the most important precautions is to consult with a healthcare professional or a qualified herbalist before administering Entada phaseoloides, especially if you have any underlying medical conditions or are taking prescription medications. This is because Entada phaseoloides can interact with certain medications, such as blood thinners and diabetes medications, and may also exacerbate certain health conditions like kidney stones and gallstones. Additionally, pregnant and breastfeeding women should exercise extreme caution when using Entada phaseoloides, as it may stimulate uterine contractions and potentially harm the fetus or baby. Furthermore, individuals with bleeding disorders or who are prone to bleeding should avoid using Entada phaseoloides, as it has been known to cause excessive bleeding in some cases. Finally, proper dosing and preparation of Entada phaseoloides are essential, as excessive consumption can lead to adverse effects such as nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea. By taking these precautions, individuals can minimize the risk of adverse reactions and maximize the potential benefits of using Entada phaseoloides medicinally.
section_horticulture: Entada phaseoloides, a species of legume tree, thrives in tropical and subtropical regions characterized by warm temperatures, high humidity, and well-defined wet and dry seasons. The ideal horticultural conditions for this plant are found in areas with an annual rainfall of 1,500-2,000 mm, with the majority of this rain falling during the summer months. The tree prefers full sun to partial shade, growing best in areas with filtered sunlight or under a canopy layer. Soils with good drainage and moderate fertility support optimal growth, although Entada phaseoloides can tolerate a range of soil types, including sand, loam, and clay. The plant is sensitive to frost and requires protection from extreme temperatures, making it unsuitable for cultivation in areas prone to frequent or severe frosts. Overall, Entada phaseoloides is adapted to environments with warm, humid conditions, moderate rainfall, and well-drained soils, making it a popular species among horticulturists and botanists studying tropical ecosystems.
section_horticulture_growth: Entada phaseoloides, a perennial climber, requires specific growth conditions to thrive. It prefers well-draining acidic to neutral soil pH ranging from 5.5 to 7.0, and can grow in a variety of soil textures, including sand, loam, and clay. The plant is adapted to tropical environments with high temperatures, typically above 64°F (18°C), and average annual rainfall of at least 30 inches (760 mm). It thrives in full sun to partial shade, with moderate to high humidity levels. Entada phaseoloides is a vigorous climber that can grow up to 20 meters (66 feet) tall, so it requires a sturdy support structure, such as a trellis or arbor, to spread its long stems and produce its showy yellow flowers and pods. Regular watering is necessary, especially during the fruiting stage, but care should be taken not to overwater, which can lead to root rot and other problems. With suitable growth conditions, Entada phaseoloides can become a stunning and productive addition to a tropical or subtropical garden.
section_horticulture_planting: Entada phaseoloides, a climbing legume also known as the "sensitive plant" or "jungle bean", requires specific planting tips to thrive. The seeds should be sown in well-draining soil with a pH range of 6.0-7.0, and a warm temperature between 65°F to 85°F (18°C to 30°C). It is essential to provide support for the seedling as it grows rapidly and can twine up to 10 meters high. Planting in a shaded area or under a canopy of larger trees will help protect the plant from strong sunlight, which can cause leaf scorch. Watering should be consistent and abundant, but not waterlogged, as Entada phaseoloides is sensitive to overwatering. Additionally, it's crucial to maintain humidity by mulching around the base of the plant or using a drip irrigation system to conserve moisture. With proper care, Entada phaseoloides will produce its distinctive white flowers and edible pods in about 6-8 months.
section_horticulture_caring: Entada phaseoloides, a species of legume native to tropical Africa and Asia, requires specific care to thrive. To ensure its optimal growth and survival, it is essential to provide it with well-draining soil that is rich in organic matter. The plant prefers partial shade to full sun, making it adaptable to various environments. Watering should be done moderately, as excessive moisture can lead to root rot. Fertilization is crucial, but over-fertilization can cause harm; a balanced diet of nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium is recommended. Pruning is also necessary to maintain the plant's shape and promote healthy growth. Remove any dead or damaged leaves and stems, and cut back long vines to encourage bushy growth. Regular monitoring for pests and diseases is essential, as Entada phaseoloides is susceptible to aphids, mealybugs, and fungal infections. By following these caring tips, gardeners can successfully cultivate this beautiful and unique plant, enjoying its stunning foliage and attractive seed pods.
section_horticulture_harvesting: Entada phaseoloides, a vining legume native to tropical Africa and Asia, requires careful consideration when it comes to harvesting. To ensure maximum yields and quality, farmers should harvest the pods when they are mature but still green, as they will continue to ripen off the vine. The pods should be checked regularly for ripeness, as over-ripeness can lead to seed spoilage and reduced germination rates. Harvesting should be done by cutting the vines just above a node or a branch point, leaving enough stem length to allow for proper drying and storage. It is essential to handle the harvested pods gently to avoid damaging them, which can compromise seed quality. Additionally, farmers should ensure that the harvested pods are dried thoroughly to a moisture level of around 15% before storing them in a well-ventilated area to prevent spoilage. Proper harvesting and handling techniques can significantly impact the quality and yield of Entada phaseoloides, making it essential for farmers to follow these tips to get the most out of their crop.
section_horticulture_pests_diseases: Entada phaseoloides, a climbing legume species native to tropical Africa and Asia, is susceptible to various pests and diseases that can significantly impact its growth and productivity. One of the most significant pests affecting E. phaseoloides is the pod borer (Maruca vitrata), which feeds on the seeds and pods, causing significant damage and reducing yields. Additionally, the legume is also prone to infestation by aphids, whiteflies, and caterpillars, which can spread plant viruses such as bean yellow mosaic virus (BYMV) and cowpea mild mottle virus (CPMMV). Fungal diseases like root rot caused by Fusarium oxysporum and leaf spot disease caused by Cercospora sp. are also common problems for E. phaseoloides, while bacterial blight caused by Xanthomonas campestris is another significant threat. Furthermore, nematodes such as Meloidogyne incognita can infect the roots of the plant, reducing its ability to absorb water and nutrients, thereby affecting its overall growth and productivity.
section_botany: Entada phaseoloides, a species of climbing legume, exhibits several distinct botanical characteristics. The plant's stems and branches are covered in soft, brown or grayish-brown hairs, giving them a velvety appearance. Its leaves are pinnately compound, with 2-6 pairs of leaflets that are elliptical to lanceolate in shape and have a pointed tip. Each leaflet measures around 7-20 cm long and 3-8 cm wide, with a soft, hairy texture and a prominent midvein. The flowers are small, yellowish-white or greenish-yellow in color, and are borne in clusters along the stems. Each flower is composed of five petals and has a distinctive shape, resembling a pea flower. The pods, which contain 2-6 seeds each, are long and flat, measuring around 10-30 cm in length and 2-3 cm in width. They are often bright green when immature, turning brown or reddish-brown at maturity.
section_botany_taxonomy: Entada phaseoloides belongs to the family Fabaceae, which is also known as Leguminosae or pea family. Specifically, it falls under the subfamily Faboideae and tribe Phaseoleae. Within this classification, E. phaseoloides is considered a species of flowering plant in the genus Entada, which consists of approximately 30-40 species native to tropical Africa, Asia, and Australia.
section_botany_variants: Entada phaseoloides has variants, such as E. phaseoloides var. pubescens, which is characterized by its hairy stem and leaves, and E. phaseoloides var. glabra, which is distinguished by its smooth stems and leaves. Another variant is E. phaseoloides subsp. macrocarpa, which is known for its larger seeds and longer pods. Additionally, there is also a forma that is often referred to as E. phaseoloides f. inermis, which has spineless stems and leaves. These variants can be found in different parts of the world, including tropical regions of Asia, Africa, and South America.
section_botany_distribution: Entada phaseoloides, also known as the Jesse tree or elephant ear, is a climbing legume native to tropical regions. Its geographic distribution spans across Africa, Asia, and the Pacific Islands, including countries such as South Africa, Mozambique, Madagascar, India, Sri Lanka, Malaysia, Indonesia, and the Philippines. It can be found growing wild in moist forests, savannas, and grasslands, often climbing trees or forming dense thickets on the forest floor. Its distribution extends from sea level to elevations of up to 1,500 meters above sea level, making it a common sight in many tropical regions worldwide.
section_botany_life_cycle: Entada phaseoloides, also known as the "stink bean" or "entada", is a legume that has a unique life-cycle. It begins its life as a seed, which germinates into a vine that grows up to 10 meters in length. The vine produces trifoliate leaves and produces small white flowers after about 3-4 months. After pollination, the flowers develop into long pods containing 2-5 seeds each. The pods mature on the plant over the next few weeks, turning from green to brown, and then dry up, releasing the seeds. The entire life-cycle of Entada phaseoloides typically takes around 6-7 months.
section_history: Entada phaseoloides, a climbing legume native to tropical Africa, has been used for centuries in various traditional and medicinal purposes. In West African countries, it was employed as a natural dye to color fabrics, while its seeds were consumed as a source of protein and fiber. Additionally, the plant's bark and leaves were used to treat ailments such as fever, cough, and skin conditions.
section_history_mythology: Entada phaseoloides, a species of legume, is steeped in mythological significance in various cultures. In Indian mythology, it is associated with Lord Hanuman, who used its pods as drums to create a mighty noise that shattered the rocks on which his teacher, Ravana, sat. Similarly, in African folklore, Entada phaseoloides is believed to have been used by the gods to summon thunderstorms and create new life. Its unique shape and size have captivated human imagination for centuries, inspiring myths and legends across cultures.
section_history_simbology: Entada phaseoloides, a type of legume native to tropical Africa, holds significant symbolic meanings in various cultures. The pods' striking resemblance to a horse's tail has led some African societies to associate it with fertility, prosperity, and abundance. In other traditions, the plant is revered for its ability to thrive in challenging environments, symbolizing resilience and adaptability.
section_history_literature: Entada phaseoloides, a species of legume native to tropical Africa and Asia, has been documented in various historical texts. The earliest written record of E. phaseoloides dates back to 1500 BCE in ancient Egyptian papyri, where it was described as a food source for humans and animals. In the 16th century, European explorers wrote about its use in traditional medicine in Africa and Southeast Asia. Additionally, the species is mentioned in the works of botanist Carl Linnaeus in the 18th century, solidifying its place in botanical history.
section_history_artifacts: Entada phaseoloides, also known as the jungle bean or coral bean, is a species of legume that has been an important part of human history and culture. Its seeds have been used as beads, ornaments, and ceremonial objects in many ancient societies, particularly in Africa, Asia, and Latin America. Historical artifacts featuring Entada phaseoloides include decorative bead necklaces from African cultures, ornate headdresses from Mesoamerican civilizations, and even ceremonial masks from pre-Columbian societies.
intro_desc: Entada phaseoloides, commonly known as fish bean, is a herbaceous plant with various health properties that have been used to improve cardiovascular health, reduce inflammation, and lower cholesterol levels. Native to tropical regions, this plant is widely cultivated in warm climates for its ornamental value and as a natural remedy. From a botanical perspective, Entada phaseoloides belongs to the Fabaceae family and is characterized by its unique, bean-like pods and compound leaves. Historically, the herb has been referenced in ancient Ayurvedic and Chinese medical texts, where it was valued for its medicinal properties and versatility in traditional healing practices.
medicine_desc: Entada phaseoloides helps with various health conditions, including fever, rheumatism, and skin problems. Its medicinal properties are attributed to its ability to reduce inflammation and pain. Traditional healers use it to treat various ailments.

The plant's medicinal properties are due to the presence of alkaloids, glycosides, and saponins. These compounds are responsible for its anti-inflammatory and analgesic effects. Studies have isolated specific compounds, such as entadine and entaphasine, which exhibit significant pharmacological activity.

The parts of the plant most used for medicinal purposes are the seeds, leaves, and roots. The seeds are rich in alkaloids, while the leaves and roots contain glycosides and saponins. These compounds are extracted and used to prepare various herbal remedies.

Improper use of Entada phaseoloides can lead to side effects, including gastrointestinal problems, allergic reactions, and liver damage. Prolonged use can also cause dependence and interact with other medications.

Precautions when using Entada phaseoloides medicinally include consulting a healthcare professional, monitoring dosage, and avoiding use during pregnancy and breastfeeding. It is also essential to note that its interaction with other medications and potential side effects can vary depending on individual circumstances.
horticulture_desc: Entada phaseoloides grow best in well-drained soil with a pH between 6.0 and 7.0. It requires full sun to partial shade and tolerates a wide range of temperatures from 10°C to 35°C. High humidity is beneficial for growth.

Planting tips for Entada phaseoloides include sowing seeds 2-3 cm deep in a nursery bed and transplanting seedlings after 6-8 weeks. Space plants 30-60 cm apart and water thoroughly after transplanting. Mulch around plants to retain moisture and suppress weeds.

Harvesting of Entada phaseoloides occurs when the pods turn yellow and split open. The seeds are mature and ready to be collected. Cut the stems of the plant near the base to facilitate harvesting and avoid damaging the plant. Seeds can be dried in the sun or using a drying machine.

Pests and diseases that commonly affect Entada phaseoloides include pod borer, leaf spot, and powdery mildew. Regular monitoring and removal of infested or infected parts can help control pest and disease outbreaks. Use organic or integrated pest management methods to minimize harm to the environment.
botany_desc: Entada phaseoloides is a climbing legume with a woody stem, hairy branches, and pinnate leaves with 5-7 leaflets, 5-12 cm long, and 3-5 cm wide. The leaves are elliptic, with a pointed tip and a serrated margin. The inflorescence is a raceme, bearing white or purple flowers.

Entada phaseoloides is classified in the family Fabaceae, subfamily Faboideae, tribe Fabeae, and is a member of the genus Entada. The plant's scientific name is Entada phaseoloides (L.) Merxm., and it is also known as Entada phaseolodes. The taxonomic classification is as follows: Kingdom: Plantae, Clade: Angiosperms, Clade: Eudicots, Clade: Rosids.

Several variants of Entada phaseoloides have been identified, including E. p. var. glabra, E. p. var. tomentosa, and E. p. var. glandulosa. These variants differ in their leaf and stem characteristics, such as hairiness and leaf shape. They are found in various parts of the plant's range.

Entada phaseoloides is native to tropical and subtropical regions of Asia and Africa, and has been naturalized in other parts of the world. It is commonly found in forests, woodlands, and grasslands, often growing on hillsides, riverbanks, and along roads.

The life cycle of Entada phaseoloides is annual, with a single growing season. The plant germinates in spring, and grows rapidly, producing flowers and seeds in summer. The seeds are released in autumn, and the plant dies back in winter, allowing the cycle to repeat.
history_desc: Entada phaseoloides is a leguminous vine native to tropical regions. Its seeds have been used for centuries in traditional medicine, food, and rituals. In ancient India, it was used as a treatment for various ailments, including fever and skin conditions.

In Hindu mythology, Entada phaseoloides is associated with the god Shiva, who is said to have used its seeds as an offering to the goddess Parvati. In ancient Greece, the plant was known as "Hippodamia" and was linked to the myth of Hippodamia, the daughter of King Augeas. The plant's seeds were also used as a charm to protect against evil spirits.

In various cultures, Entada phaseoloides has been imbued with symbolic meanings. In China, it represents abundance and good fortune, while in Africa, it symbolizes fertility and prosperity. In some cultures, its seeds are considered a talisman to ward off evil spirits and misfortune.

The historical texts of Entada phaseoloides can be found in ancient Indian and Chinese texts, such as the Ayurvedic text "Charaka Samhita" and the Chinese text "Shennong Ben Cao Jing". These texts describe the plant's medicinal properties, uses, and preparations. The texts also highlight the plant's versatility and its ability to adapt to different environments.

Historical artifacts related to Entada phaseoloides can be found in ancient temples and ruins in India and Southeast Asia. For example, the temple of Angkor Wat in Cambodia features carvings of the plant's seeds and vines. Similarly, ancient artifacts from the Indus Valley Civilization, such as pottery and jewelry, feature depictions of the plant's seeds and leaves.
